title: How I use AI as a Software Engineer
author: Luca Palminteri
text:
How I use AI as a Software Engineer
Practical workflows I actually use in my daily work
Intro
I started programming about ten years ago, long before AI was part of a developer’s daily toolkit. Back then, learning meant documentation, blog posts, asking and reading Stack Overflow, trial and error, and a lot of time being stuck.
Today, the tools are very different.
I’m a software engineer, and like many others, I’ve slowly integrated AI into my daily workflow. Not as a replacement for thinking, but as a way to learn faster, unblock myself sooner, and spend more time on the parts of the job that actually matter.
I’m writing this out of curiosity. I want to reflect on how my workflow has changed, compare what programming looked like before and after AI, and share how I personally use these tools today, in case it’s useful for other engineers navigating the same shift.
My core AI use cases
These are the areas where AI consistently adds value to my workflow. Not experimental use cases, but the ones I rely on regularly and intentionally.
Learning/understanding concepts
I usually start my AI usage by defining a fixed set of rules for the model. In tools like ChatGPT, this lives in the custom instructions section, where I specify how I want the AI to behave across conversations.
One of those rules is simple: explain things to me first. I ask the model to act like a professor or a senior engineer and break down concepts in detail before jumping into solutions.
This is my primary use of AI. I rely on it mostly for learning/understanding new ideas, unfamiliar systems, or complex problems, rather than for producing final answers.
Writing/refactoring code
There are cases where I already know what needs to be built, but the work is repetitive or verbose; unit tests, mock data, or small scripts, for example. In those situations, I describe the goal and constraints in detail and let AI generate a first draft.
I always read the output carefully, make changes, and fix mistakes if needed. The value for me is speed and iteration, not blind acceptance.
Debugging and explaining errors
This has been a big shift compared to how I used to debug. Before AI, fixing an error often meant searching for the exact error message online, sometimes copy-pasting parts of it into quotes just to find a matching result.
Now, I paste the error into the AI, ask for an explanation, and iterate from there. Even when the fix isn’t perfect, I reach the root cause much faster.
Writing (docs, PRs, git commits)
This is probably the most valuable use case for me. I strongly believe in documentation, so I try to document almost everything I do. I keep personal notes to track what I’m learning, and I use AI to help refine structure, clarity, and wording.
A recent concrete example was improving our team’s pull request template and standardizing git commit messages. AI helped me iterate faster and think more clearly about how we communicate changes.
What AI is bad at (for me)
As useful as AI is in my daily workflow, there are areas where I’ve learned to be especially careful. These are the situations where AI tends to fail me the most.
Overconfidence
AI often answers with a level of confidence that feels reassuring, but that confidence isn’t always earned. Even when it’s wrong, the explanation can sound polished and convincing. I’ve learned that a confident answer is not the same as a correct one, and this is where slowing down and verifying becomes critical. For this point, I generally add an instruction or rule so the answers feel more realistic.
Hallucinations
One of the most common issues I run into is hallucinated information: APIs that don’t exist, configuration options that sound real, or subtle details that are simply made up. This usually happens around edge cases or poorly documented features, and it’s something I now actively watch for when reviewing AI-generated output. This is the famous any type in typescript, because the AI will do things to just work.
Context blindness
AI doesn’t have access to the full context of my projects. It doesn’t know the history behind certain decisions, the trade-offs we’ve already made, or the constraints imposed by the business or the team. Because of that, its suggestions can be technically valid but practically wrong.
Subtle business logic
This is where AI struggles the most. Business rules often live between the lines or inside conversations, assumptions, and domain knowledge that never fully makes it into the code. AI can miss these nuances entirely, which makes it risky to rely on it for logic that has real-world consequences.
Rules I follow when using AI
Over time, I’ve settled on a few rules that guide how I use AI in my daily work. They’re not about efficiency as much as they are about responsibility.
Never copy blindly
If I don’t understand what the code or explanation is doing, I don’t use it. Copying and pasting might save time in the moment, but it almost always creates problems later. AI is there to assist my thinking, not replace it.
Always verify
Every AI output is a suggestion, not a source of truth. I verify behavior by running the code, checking the documentation, and reasoning through the logic myself. At the end of the day, I’m the one accountable for what ships.
Ask better prompts, not longer ones
I’ve learned that clarity beats verbosity. Short, well scoped prompts that describe intent, constraints, and expectations tend to produce better results than long, unfocused instructions. Asking the right question matters more than adding more words.
Final thoughts
Using AI hasn’t changed what I do as a software engineer, but it has changed how I think while doing it. I spend less time stuck on surface-level problems and more time reasoning about trade-offs, intent, and impact. In that sense, AI hasn’t made me faster at writing code it’s made me faster at understanding.
At the same time, it’s forced me to be more deliberate. Because answers come so easily, the cost of trusting them blindly is higher. Using AI well requires judgment, context, and ownership in ways that weren’t as visible before.
If there’s one takeaway I’d leave other engineers with, it’s this: treat AI as a thinking partner, not a shortcut. The value isn’t in what it generates, but in how it helps you refine your own understanding. The responsibility and the craft still belong to us.
Thanks for reading!
